claim: BERT pre-trains deep bidirectional representations from unlabeled text by jointly conditioning on left and right context in all layers.
evidence: Direct statement in the abstract and §3.
comment: Core contribution. Replicated thousands of times.

claim: BERT is pre-trained using masked language modeling (MLM) and next-sentence prediction (NSP).
evidence: Section 3.1 introduces both objectives with concrete formulations.

claim: BERT achieves 80.5% on GLUE, a 7.7% absolute improvement over prior state of the art.
evidence: Table 1 reports BERT_LARGE 80.5% on GLUE test set, with the previous best listed for comparison.

claim: BERT achieves SQuAD v1.1 Test F1 93.2 and SQuAD v2.0 Test F1 83.1.
evidence: Table 2 lists the Test F1 numbers for BERT_LARGE on both SQuAD versions.

claim: Bidirectionality is critical — the left-to-right-only ablation drops GLUE score by a large margin.
evidence: Table 4 compares BERT_BASE (bidirectional) to a left-to-right-only ablation; the LTR version drops GLUE by ~4 points and degrades feature-based SQuAD more.

claim: BERT is conceptually simple and empirically powerful.
BERT's [MASK] token never appears in fine-tuning data, which is a small but real pre-train/fine-tune mismatch.
section_or_page: §3.1, discussion of MLM; also Appendix A.2
evidence: The paper acknowledges the [MASK] mismatch as a limitation that could affect performance.

claim: BERT_LARGE has 24 layers, 1024 hidden units, 16 attention heads, and 340M parameters.
evidence: The model description table in §3 lists BERT_LARGE hyperparameters.

claim: Fine-tuning is much cheaper than pre-training from scratch on the downstream task.
evidence: The paper states fine-tuning takes 2–3 epochs on a single TPU and is orders of magnitude cheaper than pre-training.

claim: BERT fixed max sequence length is 512 tokens (inherited from the Transformer).
evidence: The learned position embeddings are described as 512 tokens in §3.
